does: This paper proposes a class of adversarial attacks that eliminates the computationally expensive backward pass by predicting input gradients directly from forward-pass hidden states using lightweight linear regression. The key innovation is training a simple linear model to map hidden representations to gradients, exploiting the kernel view of neural networks where this mapping is theoretically exact in the Neural Tangent Kernel regime. Empirically, the method recovers most of FGSM's attack effectiveness while requiring only a fraction of the computational time, achieving a 5.32× throughput improvement. The approach is evaluated on standard benchmarks and demonstrates practical speedups under realistic wall-clock constraints while maintaining competitive attack success rates.
connects: This work builds on the long line of fast adversarial attack research, particularly extending FGSM and its variants that form the foundation of efficient robustness evaluation. It connects to recent theoretical understanding of neural networks through the Neural Tangent Kernel lens, which has illuminated how overparameterized networks behave. The paper also relates to gradient-based adversarial training methods, which require generating attacks at scale and thus are bottlenecked by backward-pass costs. Additionally, it engages with the broader literature on interpretability and understanding neural network internals through hidden representations, leveraging the idea that gradients can be predicted from intermediate features rather than computed explicitly.
enables: This work opens avenues for scaling adversarial training and robustness evaluation to larger models and datasets where backward-pass costs become prohibitive. The gradient prediction framework could enable real-time adversarial red-teaming and online robustness monitoring in deployed systems. Future work could extend the approach to other gradient-dependent robustness techniques, certified defenses, and potentially to other domains where gradient computation is expensive. The connection to kernel methods suggests deeper theoretical investigation into when and why gradient prediction succeeds, potentially leading to better predictors or hybrid approaches. More broadly, it demonstrates that critical ML pipeline components need not rely on traditional backpropagation, opening design space for fundamentally faster training and evaluation procedures.
